ep01_intro: ^The clock on the wall reads 2:47 AM.
^The room smells of stale coffee and warm electronics. Your shift shouldn't have started for another hour, but sleep stopped coming three weeks ago — around the same time the letters started.
^You scan the dial out of habit. Static. White noise. The comfortable meaninglessness of dead air.
^A pattern buried in the hiss. Too regular to be random. Too fragile to be a broadcast.
ep01_signal_close: ^You lean in. The signal pulses — three short, three long, three short — then dissolves back into static.
^Morse. Someone is out there, broadcasting on a frequency that, according to every chart you own, doesn't exist.
ep01_signal_ignore: ^Something keeps you exactly where you are. The signal pulses — three short, three long, three short — before fading.
ep01_signal_response: ^You grab your log and write down the frequency: 156.925 MHz. The maritime emergency band — except you're four hundred kilometres from the nearest ocean.
^Before you can reach for the transmitter, a voice cuts through.
^"Hello? Is someone there? Please — I don't have much time."
ep01_respond_fast: ^"I'm here. I can hear you. What's happening?"
ep01_respond_careful: ^You hit record on the old tape deck before picking up the transmitter.
^"Signal received. Identifying myself — I'm at the Harrow Point relay station. Who is this?"
ep01_respond_silent: ^You press record and say nothing. The signal is evidence. Witnesses make mistakes; recordings don't.
^"I know someone is there. The frequency doesn't allow for echo. Please."
ep01_alex_replies: ^"Thank God. Listen — my name is Alex. Alex Voss. I'm sending this from— "
^Static swallows the rest of the sentence. When the voice returns, it has changed. Colder. Faster.
^"They're going to find this channel soon. I need you to write down what I'm about to tell you. Every word. Can you do that?"
^I need your full name and location before I proceed.
ep01_alex_message_who: ^"Before I write anything down — who are 'they'?"
^A pause. Long enough to feel deliberate.
^"If I tell you that now, you'll think I'm unstable. Write first. Judge later. Please."
ep01_alex_message_formal: ^"Protocol requires full identification before I can acknowledge an emergency signal."
^"Protocol." A short, humourless sound. "All right. Alex Voss. Location — I genuinely cannot tell you that safely. Now. Please."
ep01_alex_transmits: ^"The Meridian Project is not what it claims to be. The data they published — the trial results — they've been altered. I have the original files. If something happens to me, look for the name 'Halcyon' in the Voss Institute records. That's where the truth is buried."
^"I'm almost out of time. One last thing —
^, is it? Don't trust anyone who mentions the Lighthouse Protocol first. Anyone. Not even—"
^You sit alone in the pre-dawn dark with a notebook full of words that feel like a lit fuse.
ep01_ending: ^Outside, the first grey light of morning is beginning to separate the sky from the fields.

b: ^You caught every word of it — the signal, the message, the name. Whatever comes next, you have a head start.
^Most of it, anyway. There's a gap in the middle that nags at you.
^The voice felt real to you. Frightened, yes — but truthful. That counts for something.
^You can't shake the feeling you handled that poorly. If Alex Voss is genuine, you may have cost yourself an ally.

ep01_ending: ^You don't know yet whether finding that name will save you or destroy you.
